title: What Calls and Puts Are
html:
A contract, not a share

A stock share is a piece of a company. An option is something different: it is a contract between two people about a stock, with a deadline. Owning an option does not make you an owner of the company. It gives you a specific right for a specific window of time, and that right has a price. There are exactly two flavors, and almost everything in options is built from them.

The call: the right to BUY

A call option gives its owner the right, but not the obligation, to buy 100 shares of a stock at a fixed price (the strike) on or before a fixed date (the expiration). You buy a call when you think the stock is going up. The strike is the price you have locked in to pay; if the stock rises far above it, you can still buy cheaply at the strike and pocket the difference.

The put: the right to SELL

A put option gives its owner the right, but not the obligation, to sell 100 shares at the strike on or before expiration. You buy a put when you think the stock is going down, or to protect shares you own. If the stock collapses, you can still sell at the high strike you locked in.

Definition.
Call = right to BUY at strike K by expiration. Put = right to SELL at strike K by expiration. One contract controls 100 shares, so a quoted price of $3.00 costs $3.00 × 100 = $300.

The four things every option contract names

  • Underlying — which stock (e.g., ACME).
  • Type — call or put.
  • Strike — the fixed transaction price, e.g., 100.
  • Expiration — the deadline, e.g., the third Friday of next month.

So "the ACME June 100 call" is a single, fully-specified contract: the right to buy ACME at $100 until June expiration. The price you pay for that right is the premium.

Worked example: a 100-strike call when the stock runs

ACME trades at $98. You buy one 100-strike call for a premium of $3.00, costing $300. You now own the right to buy 100 shares at $100 anytime before expiration. Suppose ACME rallies to $115.

ACME 100 call · stock runs to 115
Right granted: buy 100 shares at $100 Stock at expiration: $115 Exercise value per share: 115 − 100 = $15 Value of the contract: $15 × 100 = $1,500 Premium paid: $300 Net profit: 1,500 − 300 = $1,200

You paid $300 to control 100 shares. A $17 move in the stock (from 98 to 115) turned into a $1,200 gain — roughly 400% on the premium. That leverage is why options attract traders. But notice the asymmetry: if ACME had instead drifted to $99 and stayed there, the right to buy at 100 would be worthless and you would lose the entire $300. The next lesson makes that risk profile precise.

Read the diagram left to right. Below the $100 strike the line is flat and negative — the call expires worthless and you lose the $3 premium no matter how far the stock falls. At $103 the line crosses zero (strike plus premium). Above that, every dollar the stock gains adds a dollar of profit, and the line never stops climbing. That single hockey-stick shape is the foundation of everything in this part.

▸Try It

Goal: translate a directional view into the right contract.

  1. NOVA trades at $50. You believe it will rise to $60. State which option type you would buy and a sensible strike.
  2. Now you believe NOVA will fall to $40. State the type and strike for that view.
  3. For a 50-strike call bought at $2.00, compute the contract value if NOVA finishes at $58, and your net profit.

title: Rights of the Buyer vs Obligations of the Seller
html:
Every option has two sides

For every option contract that exists, someone bought it and someone sold it. The buyer and seller are mirror images with opposite rights, opposite obligations, and opposite cash flows. Confusing the two is the costliest beginner mistake in options, so let us nail it down.

The buyer (long): pays premium, holds a RIGHT

The option buyer is said to be long the option. They pay the premium up front. In exchange they hold a right they may or may not use. Because it is a right and not a duty, the buyer can simply walk away — let the option expire — if it is not worth exercising. That is what caps their downside.

  • Cash flow: pays premium now (a debit).
  • Maximum loss: the premium paid — and not a penny more.
  • Maximum gain: large (unlimited for a long call as the stock rises; very large for a long put down to zero).
  • Wants: the option to finish deep in-the-money.

The seller (short): collects premium, holds an OBLIGATION

The option seller — also called the writer — is short the option. They receive the premium immediately. In return they take on an obligation: if the buyer chooses to exercise, the seller must perform. A call writer must deliver (sell) 100 shares at the strike. A put writer must buy 100 shares at the strike. The seller has no choice in the matter — the buyer holds the decision.

  • Cash flow: receives premium now (a credit).
  • Maximum gain: the premium collected — and not a penny more.
  • Maximum loss: large (potentially unlimited for a naked short call).
  • Wants: the option to expire worthless so the premium is kept free and clear.
Definition.
Buyer pays premium → owns a right → loss capped at premium. Seller receives premium → owes an obligation → gain capped at premium. The two payoffs are exact mirror images across the horizontal axis.

Worked example: the same 100 call from both seats

ACME is at $99. The 100 call trades at $3.00. Trader B buys one; Trader S sells one. They are in the same trade, facing each other.

Same 100 call · two seats · three outcomes
Premium: Buyer pays $300, Seller receives $300 Stock ends $95 (OTM): Buyer loses $300 · Seller keeps $300 Stock ends $103 (BE): intrinsic $300; Buyer $0 · Seller $0 Stock ends $115: intrinsic $1,500; Buyer +$1,200 · Seller −$1,200 Stock ends $140: intrinsic $4,000; Buyer +$3,700 · Seller −$3,700

Look at the bottom rows. As ACME climbs, the buyer's profit and the seller's loss grow together, dollar for dollar, with no ceiling. The seller collected $300 but is now obligated to deliver 100 shares worth $140 each for only $100 each — a $4,000 outlay against $300 received. This is the defining trade-off: sellers win small and often (keeping premium when options expire worthless) but expose themselves to large, sometimes unlimited losses; buyers lose small and often but hold the lottery-ticket upside.

Notice this short-call diagram is the previous lesson's long call flipped upside down. Below $100 the seller keeps the full $3 premium (flat green). Above $103 — the same break-even — the line plunges, and it keeps falling without bound. The buyer's hockey stick and the seller's inverted hockey stick are the same line reflected across zero, which is just the accounting truth that one side's gain is the other's loss.

Pitfall.
Selling options feels like "easy income" because premium lands in your account instantly. But a single uncovered short call in a runaway stock can lose multiples of every premium you ever collected. Never confuse the small, certain credit with the size of the obligation behind it.
▸Try It

Goal: compute both sides of one contract.

  1. NOVA 50 put trades at $2.50. Write the buyer's debit and the seller's credit in dollars.
  2. NOVA finishes at $42. Compute the put's intrinsic value, then the buyer's net P/L and the seller's net P/L.
  3. In one sentence, state which outcome the put seller is rooting for and why.

title: Moneyness: In, At and Out of the Money
html:
Where the strike sits relative to the stock

Moneyness describes the relationship between an option's strike and the current stock price. It tells you, right now, whether the option has any exercise value and how it will behave. The vocabulary is universal: in-the-money (ITM), at-the-money (ATM), and out-of-the-money (OTM). Calls and puts use the same words but in opposite directions, so be careful.

Calls: ITM when the strike is BELOW the stock

A call is the right to buy at the strike. That right is valuable when the strike is cheaper than the market — when the stock is above the strike.

  • ITM call: strike below stock (you could buy below market).
  • ATM call: strike roughly equal to the stock.
  • OTM call: strike above stock (no point buying above market).

Puts: ITM when the strike is ABOVE the stock

A put is the right to sell at the strike. That right is valuable when the strike is higher than the market — when the stock is below the strike.

  • ITM put: strike above stock (you could sell above market).
  • ATM put: strike roughly equal to the stock.
  • OTM put: strike below stock (no point selling below market).
Definition.
Call intrinsic = max(S − K, 0). Put intrinsic = max(K − S, 0), where S is the stock price and K the strike. An option is ITM when its intrinsic value is positive, OTM when intrinsic is zero, ATM when S ≈ K.

Worked example: ACME at $103

The stock sits at $103. Let us classify a ladder of strikes for both calls and puts.

Moneyness map · ACME = $103
95 call: 103 − 95 = $8 intrinsic → ITM · 95 put → OTM 100 call: 103 − 100 = $3 intrinsic → ITM · 100 put → OTM 103 call: 103 − 103 = $0 → ATM · 103 put → ATM 110 call: below stock? no → OTM · 110 put: 110 − 103 = $7 → ITM 120 call: OTM · 120 put: 120 − 103 = $17 → deep ITM

The skeleton's example reads off this table directly: with ACME at 103, the 100 call is ITM (you can buy $3 under market), the 103 call is ATM, and the 110 call is OTM. Flip to puts and the picture inverts — the 110 put is the ITM one because you would happily sell at 110 when the market is only 103.

The horizontal lines are strikes; the candles are the stock. Any call strike that sits below the price line is in-the-money, and the further below, the more intrinsic value it carries. As the stock trends up through the chart, strikes that began OTM (like 110) move toward and eventually into the money. Moneyness is therefore not fixed — it migrates every time the stock moves, which is exactly why an option's value changes with price.

Insight.
Deep-ITM options behave almost like the stock itself (they move nearly dollar-for-dollar). Far-OTM options behave like cheap lottery tickets — small absolute price, large percentage swings, and a high chance of expiring worthless. ATM options are the most sensitive to time and volatility.
▸Try It

Goal: classify a strike ladder fast.

  1. NOVA = $72. For strikes 65, 70, 72, 75, 80, label each call as ITM, ATM, or OTM.
  2. For the same five strikes, label each put.
  3. Compute the intrinsic value of the 65 call and the 80 put.

title: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Value and Time Decay
html:
Every premium splits into two parts

An option's price — its premium — is never one undifferentiated number. It always decomposes cleanly into two pieces: intrinsic value (the exercise value baked in right now) and extrinsic value (everything else — the value of time and uncertainty). Understanding this split is what separates traders who know why an option costs what it does from those who just see a price.

Intrinsic value: what it is worth if exercised today

Intrinsic value is the moneyness amount from the previous lesson — the profit you would lock in by exercising immediately. It can never be negative, because you would simply decline to exercise.

Definition.
Call intrinsic = max(S − K, 0). Put intrinsic = max(K − S, 0). Extrinsic (time) value = Premium − Intrinsic. Therefore Premium = Intrinsic + Extrinsic, and Extrinsic ≥ 0 always.

Extrinsic value: the price of time and possibility

Extrinsic value is what you pay for the chance that the option becomes more valuable before expiration. It is fueled by two things: time remaining (more days = more chances to move) and implied volatility (a jumpier stock has fatter odds of a big favorable move). An OTM option has zero intrinsic value — its entire premium is extrinsic, pure hope priced by the market.

Worked example: the $6 call dissected

ACME is at $103. The 100 call trades at $6.00. Let us split it.

100 call @ $6.00 · stock $103
Intrinsic = max(103 − 100, 0) = $3.00 Premium = $6.00 Extrinsic = 6.00 − 3.00 = $3.00 Split: half real value, half time value

So the $6 premium is $3 intrinsic + $3 time value. The intrinsic $3 is solid — it tracks the stock. The extrinsic $3 is melting ice: it must decay to exactly $0 by expiration, because at expiry there is no time left and no uncertainty — the option is worth only its intrinsic value.

Time decay (theta): the daily melt

The erosion of extrinsic value as expiration approaches is called time decay, measured by the Greek theta. Crucially, decay is not linear. It is slow when expiration is distant and accelerates sharply in the final weeks, collapsing in the last days. An ATM option loses time value fastest near the end.

Read the curve right (lots of time) to left (expiration). Far from expiry the line is nearly flat — losing a day costs little. As you approach the deadline the slope steepens dramatically, and in the final stretch the remaining time value falls off a cliff to zero. This is why option buyers fight a constant headwind: even if the stock does nothing, their extrinsic value bleeds away each day, fastest at the very end. It is also why option sellers like to be short into the final weeks, harvesting that accelerating decay.

Same 100 call · stock frozen at $103 · time passes
60 days out: intrinsic $3.00 + time $3.00 = $6.00 30 days out: intrinsic $3.00 + time $2.00 = $5.00 7 days out: intrinsic $3.00 + time $0.80 = $3.80 At expiration: intrinsic $3.00 + time $0.00 = $3.00

The stock never moved, yet the option fell from $6.00 to $3.00 — a 50% loss for the buyer — purely from time decay. The intrinsic $3 survived; the extrinsic $3 evaporated. This is the single most important reason that simply being "right on direction" is not enough: you must be right before theta eats your premium.

Pitfall.
Buying cheap far-OTM options because they "only cost $0.30" ignores that 100% of that premium is extrinsic and decays to zero unless the stock makes a real move. Cheap does not mean high-probability.
▸Try It

Goal: separate the two halves of a premium.

  1. NOVA = $48. The 45 call trades at $4.20. Compute intrinsic and extrinsic value.
  2. The 50 call on the same stock trades at $1.10. What is its intrinsic value, and what fraction of its premium is time value?
  3. Explain in one sentence why the 50 call is "all theta" and what happens to it at expiration if NOVA stays at $48.

title: Expiration, Exercise and Assignment
html:
The deadline arrives

Every option has a final day. What happens at expiration — and the mechanics of exercise (the buyer using their right) and assignment (the seller being forced to perform) — trips up many beginners, partly because much of it now happens automatically. Let us walk through it step by step.

Three things can happen to an option

  1. It expires worthless. If the option is OTM at expiration, no one exercises it. It vanishes. The buyer loses the premium; the seller keeps it. This is the fate of most options.
  2. It is exercised. If the option is ITM, the holder exercises (or it is auto-exercised), converting the contract into a stock transaction at the strike.
  3. It is closed early. Before expiration, either side can usually just buy or sell the option in the market to exit, never touching shares at all. Most traders do this rather than exercise.

Automatic exercise: the $0.01 rule

At U.S. expiration, brokers and the clearinghouse (the OCC) auto-exercise any option that is ITM by $0.01 or more, unless the holder instructs otherwise. You do not have to do anything — an ITM long call will turn into 100 purchased shares whether you intended it or not.

Pitfall.
Auto-exercise can blindside small accounts. An ITM long call auto-exercises into a $10,000+ stock purchase (100 × a $100 strike) you may not have cash for. If you do not want the shares, close the option before expiration. Do not let surprise assignment do your trading for you.

Assignment: the seller's side of exercise

When a buyer exercises, the clearinghouse assigns the obligation to a randomly chosen seller of that contract. The assigned seller must perform:

  • Short call assigned: must sell (deliver) 100 shares at the strike. If they do not own the shares, they are forced to buy them at market and deliver — possibly at a steep loss.
  • Short put assigned: must buy 100 shares at the strike, paying strike × 100 in cash whether they wanted the shares or not.

Worked example: an ITM 100 call at expiry

ACME closes expiration day at $106. Trader B is long one 100 call (paid $3); Trader S is short that call (collected $3). The call is $6 ITM, so it auto-exercises.

ITM 100 call · auto-exercise at $106
Buyer receives: 100 shares at $100 (cost $10,000) Shares worth: 100 × $106 = $10,600 Buyer gain on exercise: $600 − $300 premium = +$300 net Seller delivers: 100 shares at $100, receiving $10,000 Seller must source shares worth: $10,600 Seller loss on assignment: −$600 + $300 premium = −$300 net

The buyer's $600 exercise gain exactly equals the seller's $600 delivery loss — assignment is a zero-sum handoff of 100 shares at $100. After premium, the buyer nets +$300 and the seller nets −$300. If the seller did not already own ACME (a naked short call), they were forced to buy 100 shares at $106 to deliver at $100, realizing the loss in cash.

Pin risk and weekends

When the stock closes right at the strike (a "pin"), the holder may not know whether they will be assigned until after the close — and the stock can move over the weekend before the resulting shares can be traded Monday. This pin risk is one more reason professionals often close ITM options before expiration rather than carry them into the auto-exercise process.

Trace the price into the final bars. As long as ACME settles above the 100 strike line, the call is ITM and will be exercised — the holder ends up long 100 shares at $100. Had the stock instead closed at $99, just below the line, the very same call would expire worthless and the entire premium would be lost. Expiration is a knife-edge: a few cents across the strike flips the outcome completely.

▸Try It

Goal: resolve options at expiration.

  1. NOVA closes at $47. State the expiration outcome for a long 45 call and a long 50 call (exercised or worthless).
  2. For a short 50 put on NOVA at $47, state what the seller is assigned to do and at what total cash cost.
  3. Explain why a trader holding a long ITM call but with no cash for shares might prefer to sell the option before 4 p.m. on expiration day.

title: Reading an Options Chain
html:
The menu of every contract

An options chain is the grid your broker shows for a single stock and expiration: every available strike, with calls usually on one side and puts on the other, each row carrying a handful of numbers. Learning to read it fluently is a basic literacy skill. Let us decode every column.

The columns that matter

  • Strike — the contract's fixed transaction price. Rows are sorted by strike, ITM on one side of the current price, OTM on the other.
  • Bid — the highest price a buyer in the market will pay. If you sell, you get the bid.
  • Ask (offer) — the lowest price a seller will accept. If you buy, you pay the ask.
  • Spread — ask minus bid; the round-trip cost of crossing the market. Tight spreads = liquid; wide spreads = illiquid and expensive to trade.
  • Last — the most recent trade price (can be stale).
  • Volume — contracts traded today. A fresh, daily activity gauge.
  • Open interest (OI) — the total number of contracts currently outstanding (opened but not yet closed). A cumulative measure of how many positions exist.
Definition.
Bid/ask = the prices you sell/buy at. Volume = today's trades (resets daily). Open interest = live contracts that exist right now (changes only as positions are opened or closed). Mid price ≈ (Bid + Ask) ÷ 2 is a fair estimate of true value.

Volume vs open interest — the key distinction

Beginners confuse these constantly. Volume is a flow — how many traded today, reset to zero each morning. Open interest is a stock (in the inventory sense) — how many contracts are alive across all days, updated overnight. High OI means a deep pool of existing positions and usually tight, tradeable spreads; high volume means lots of attention today. You want both to be healthy before trading a contract.

Worked example: decoding one row

ACME trades at $103. Here is the 105 call row from the chain.

ACME 105 call · row decoded · stock $103
Bid / Ask: 2.40 / 2.55 Spread: 2.55 − 2.40 = $0.15 (tight, liquid) Mid (fair value): (2.40 + 2.55) ÷ 2 = $2.475 To BUY you pay: $2.55 × 100 = $255 To SELL you get: $2.40 × 100 = $240 Volume today: 1,200 contracts Open interest: 8,400 contracts (deep, liquid) Moneyness: strike 105 > stock 103 → OTM, all $2.475 is extrinsic

Everything you need is in those numbers. The 105 call is OTM (strike above the $103 stock), so its entire $2.475 mid value is time value. The spread is just $0.15 — narrow, meaning you lose little crossing the market. The 8,400 open interest tells you thousands of these contracts already exist, so you can get in and out easily; the 1,200 volume confirms active trading today. Buy at the ask ($2.55, or $255 per contract); sell at the bid ($2.40, or $240). That $15 gap is the market-maker's edge and your transaction cost.

Insight.
When a spread is wide (say bid 2.00 / ask 2.80), never pay the ask blindly. Place a limit order near the mid; you can often get filled inside the spread, saving real money. Low OI plus a wide spread is a warning that the contract is illiquid — easy to enter, painful to exit.

The chart shows ACME ranging just under the 105 strike. Because price keeps stalling below that line, the 105 call stays OTM and its premium is all extrinsic — which decays daily. For that call to gain real (intrinsic) value, the candles must break and hold above 105. This links the chain back to the chart: a row of numbers only becomes profitable when price action carries the stock through the strike.

▸Try It

Goal: read a chain row like a pro.

  1. NOVA = $72. The 70 call shows bid 3.10 / ask 3.30. Compute the spread, the mid, and your cost to buy one contract.
  2. Is the 70 call ITM or OTM? What is its intrinsic value and its extrinsic value (using the mid)?
  3. One row has OI 12 and a 1.80/2.90 spread; another has OI 9,000 and a 2.30/2.35 spread. Which would you trade and why?

title: American vs European and Settlement
html:
When you can exercise, and what you receive

Two structural features quietly change how an option behaves: its exercise style (when the holder may exercise) and its settlement (whether exercise delivers shares or cash). Getting these wrong leads to nasty surprises — like being assigned over a holiday weekend, or expecting stock and receiving a cash debit instead.

American vs European style

The names have nothing to do with geography. They describe when the right may be used.

  • American-style: can be exercised any time up to and including expiration. Nearly all single-stock options and ETF options (e.g., on individual companies) are American. This means a short option holder can be assigned early, on any day.
  • European-style: can be exercised only at expiration. Most broad-based index options (e.g., the S&P 500 index, SPX) are European. No early assignment risk.
Definition.
American = exercisable any day through expiration (early assignment possible). European = exercisable only at expiration (no early assignment). Style is a fixed property of the contract, not a choice you make.

Physical vs cash settlement

When an option is exercised, how does the obligation get fulfilled?

  • Physical settlement: actual shares change hands at the strike. Single-stock options are physically settled — exercise the 100 call and you literally receive 100 shares for $10,000.
  • Cash settlement: no shares move; instead the in-the-money amount is paid in cash. Index options like SPX are cash-settled because you cannot deliver "the index" — there is no such share.

Worked example: stock call vs index option

Two contracts at expiration
ACME 100 call (American, physical): stock $106 → exercise → receive 100 shares at $100 ($10,000 paid) → can also be exercised early, any day SPX 5,000 call (European, cash): index settles 5,030 → ITM by 30 points × $100 multiplier = $3,000 cash → no shares; no early exercise, settles only at expiry

The ACME call hands you stock and a $10,000 bill; the SPX call simply credits $3,000 of cash and disappears — clean, no shares, no overnight stock risk after settlement. The SPX holder also never worries about being assigned on a random Tuesday, whereas an ACME short call writer must always watch for early assignment.

Why early exercise (American) usually does not happen — except around dividends

For most American calls, early exercise is irrational: exercising throws away the option's remaining time value, so it is better to sell the option than exercise it. The big exception is dividends. Just before a stock pays a dividend, a deep-ITM call holder may exercise early to capture the dividend by owning the shares on the ex-date. That is the main scenario in which short call writers get assigned ahead of expiration.

Pitfall.
If you are short an American ITM call going into an ex-dividend date, assume you may be assigned early and end up short the stock and owing the dividend. European index options remove this risk entirely — one reason index traders favor them.
Insight.
SPX (European, cash-settled) versus SPY (the ETF, American, physically settled) track nearly the same index but behave differently at the edges: SPX has no early-assignment or pin-into-shares risk, while SPY can leave you holding actual ETF shares. Same exposure, different mechanics.

As ACME trends well above the 100 strike, the call goes deep ITM and its time value shrinks toward zero. With little time value left to forfeit, capturing an upcoming dividend can make early exercise worthwhile for the holder — and that is precisely when the short call writer should expect an early assignment notice. A European contract on the same trajectory would simply sit until expiration regardless.

▸Try It

Goal: match style and settlement to a contract.

  1. For each — a single-stock NOVA call and an index SPX put — state the exercise style and settlement type.
  2. An SPX 5,000 put settles with the index at 4,940. Compute the cash settlement (multiplier $100).
  3. Explain in one sentence why a short American call writer should be on alert just before an ex-dividend date.

title: Payoffs of a Single Long/Short Call/Put
html:
The four building blocks

Every options strategy ever devised is assembled from four atoms: long call, short call, long put, short put. If you can draw each one's payoff at expiration and state its break-even, you can reason about any combination. Let us chart all four and compute their break-evens precisely.

Definition.
A payoff diagram plots profit/loss (vertical) against the stock price at expiration (horizontal). Break-even is where profit = 0. Long call BE = strike + premium. Long put BE = strike − premium. Short positions share the same break-even but mirror the profit across zero.

1. Long call — bullish, limited risk, unlimited reward

You pay premium for the right to buy. Below the strike the call expires worthless and you lose the full premium. Above the strike profit rises one-for-one with the stock, with no ceiling.

Long 100 call · premium $3
Max loss: −$300 (premium), at any price ≤ 100 Break-even: 100 + 3 = $103 At $110: (110 − 100) × 100 − 300 = +$700 Max gain: unlimited as stock rises

The hockey stick: flat at −$3 below the strike, kinking up at 100, crossing zero at the $103 break-even, then climbing forever. This is the position from Lesson 1.

2. Short call — bearish/neutral, limited reward, unlimited risk

You collect premium and take the obligation to deliver. It is the mirror of the long call.

Short 100 call · credit $3
Max gain: +$300 (premium), at any price ≤ 100 Break-even: $103 At $110: −$700 Max loss: unlimited as stock rises

Flip the long call upside down across zero and you have it. Profit is capped at the $3 premium below the strike; above $103 losses run without bound. Selling naked calls carries the most dangerous risk profile of the four.

3. Long put — bearish, limited risk, large reward

You pay premium for the right to sell. Above the strike the put expires worthless (lose premium). Below the strike profit rises as the stock falls, all the way down to zero.

Long 100 put · premium $3
Max loss: −$300 (premium), at any price ≥ 100 Break-even: 100 − 3 = $97 At $85: (100 − 85) × 100 − 300 = +$1,200 Max gain: (100 − 0) × 100 − 300 = +$9,700 at $0

A reversed hockey stick: flat −$3 above the strike, kinking down through the $97 break-even, rising leftward as the stock drops. The reward is large but bounded — a stock can only fall to zero.

4. Short put — bullish/neutral, limited reward, large risk

You collect premium and take the obligation to buy at the strike. Mirror of the long put.

Short 100 put · credit $3
Max gain: +$300 (premium), at any price ≥ 100 Break-even: $97 At $85: −$1,200 Max loss: −$9,700 if stock goes to $0

Above $97 you keep the full $3 credit; below it losses mount as the stock falls toward zero. This is the payoff behind the "cash-secured put" income strategy — you are paid $3 to stand ready to buy at $100.

Insight.
Two clean rules tie it together: (1) buyers (long) have limited risk and large reward; sellers (short) have limited reward and large risk. (2) Calls profit up, puts profit down. Long call BE = K + premium; long put BE = K − premium; the short shares the BE but flips the sign of every profit.
Pitfall.
The two short, naked positions (short call, short put) carry the outsized losses. Beginners drawn to the steady premium income must respect that one large adverse move can erase many months of collected credits.
▸Try It

Goal: draw and price all four atoms.

  1. NOVA 50 call costs $2. State max loss, break-even, and P/L at $58 for the long call and the short call.
  2. NOVA 50 put costs $2. State max loss, break-even, and P/L at $42 for the long put and the short put.
  3. For each of the four, label it bullish or bearish and limited-risk or large-risk.
